content.full_text:
July 16, 1991


Monte Widders
Myers, Widders & Gibson
5425 Everglades Street, Suite 100
Ventura, CA 93003

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-212

Dear Mr. Widders:

You have requested advice on behalf of Staal, Gardner &
Dunne, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "SG&D"), concerning
application of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ The following advice is based
upon the facts provided in your letter and the agreement between
the City of Simi Valley and SG&D, which you provided to me on
June 17, 1991.

Our advice is limited only to provisions of the Act. We
cannot provide advice about other conflict-of-interest laws, such
as Government Code Section 1090. Since this request is general in
nature, we are treating your question as a request for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).

QUESTIONS

1. Is SG&D, the contract geotechnical engineering firm to
the city, deemed a consultant for purposes of the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act?

2. Are the employees of SG&D deemed consultants for purposes
of the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act?

3. If the SG&D employees are deemed to be consultants, what
obligations do they incur under the Act?

___________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. I-90-212
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

     1.  A consultant must be a natural person and cannot be a
corporation.  Consequently, SG&D is not a "consultant" for
purposes of the Act.

     2.  The employees of SG&D who work on the city's projects may
be consultants if they provide information, advice, recommendation
or counsel to the city.  However, if the employees function
independently of the city's control and direction, and possess no
authority with respect to any city decision beyond the rendering
of information, advice, recommendation or counsel, they will not
be consultants under the Act.  We do not have sufficient
information about the relationship between the city and the
employees of SG&D.  However, it appears that the employees may be
consultants due to the long-standing contractual relationship
between SG&D and the city.  The engineers review reports and
render advice on a regular basis; their opinions and analyses have
become an integral part of the development permit review process.
If the city relies upon an engineer's expertise to approve or deny
a permit or proposed project, the engineer is considered a
consultant.

     3.  Assuming the employees of SG&D are consultants under the
Act, they are required to file statements of economic interests
pursuant to their designation in the city's conflict of interest
code.  They are also required to disqualify from participating in
any governmental decision in which they have a financial interest.

FACTS

     SG&D is a California corporation, consisting of geotechnical
engineers and geologists, which has been retained as consulting
geotechnical engineers by the City of Simi Valley (the "City")
since March 5, 1985.  As contract geotechnical engineers, SG&D
specifically "reviews geotechnical reports and studies in
accordance with the city's guidelines."  The reports are prepared
by other consultants as part of the development permit review
process.

     SG&D is asked to provide independent opinions regarding these
geotechnical reports.  However, SG&D has no specific discretion or
decision-making role or authority with respect to the approval or
denial of the proposed project itself.  The opinions rendered by
SG&D to the city are given without advice, direction or control of
the city staff.  On the other hand, the independent contractual
relationship with the city is long-standing and SG&D is called
upon to render its independent advice on a regular basis.

ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participate in making, or otherwise using his or her official

File No. I-90-212
Page 3


position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. "Public official" is defined
in Section 82048 to include every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency.

Regulation 18700 (copy enclosed) defines public official and
consultant as follows:

(a) "Public official at any level of
state or local government" means every natural
person who is a member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government
agency.

* * *

(2) "Consultant" shall include any
natural person who provides, under contract,
information, advice, recommendation or counsel
to a state or local government agency,
provided, however, that "consultant" shall not
include a person who:

(A) Conducts research and arrives
at conclusions with respect to his or her
rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel independent of
the control and direction of the agency
or of any agency official, other than
normal contract monitoring; and

(B) Possesses no authority with
respect to any agency decision beyond the
rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel.

Thus, according to Regulation 18700(a)(2), a consultant must
be a natural person and cannot be a corporation. Consequently,
SG&D, a corporation, is not a consultant for purposes of the Act.

However, the employees of SG&D who work on city projects may
be consultants, and consequently, public officials, under the Act.
The employees are considered consultants if they provide
information, advice, recommendation or counsel to the city, are
subject to the city's control and direction, or possess authority
with respect to any city decisions. (Hayden Advice Letter, No. A-
84-319, copy enclosed.) Conversely, if the employees act
independent of the city's control and direction, and possess no
authority with respect to any city decision beyond the rendition
of information, advice, recommendation or counsel, the employees
are not considered consultants. (Regulation 18700(a)(2); Hayden
Advice Letter, supra; Rose Advice Letter, No. A-84-306; and Kaplan
Advice Letter, No. A-82-108, copies enclosed.)

File No. I-90-212
Page 4


     According to the facts provided, the SG&D firm has had an
independent contractual relationship with the city for over six
years and the engineers have been providing information, advice,
recommendation or counsel to the city on a regular basis. This
long-standing relationship may provide regular contact with city
staff and officials as the engineers render ongoing advice. By
rendering ongoing advice, the SG&D engineers have become, in
essence, an extension of the city staff and their opinions and
advice are probably relied upon during the development permit
review process. (See Crisis Advice Letter, No. A-82-029.) If an
engineer provides advice or counsel or recommends a specific
course of action on a continuing basis, he or she is deemed to be
a consultant under the Act.

     We do not have complete information about the relationship
between the city and the employees of SG&D. However, it appears
that the engineers may be consultants. An engineer who is deemed
a consultant is subject to both the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act. The engineer must be
designated in the city's conflict of interest code under a
consultant category. As we previously discussed, since the city's
conflict of interest code does not have a consultant category, we
will suggest that the city amend its code.

Participate in Making a Governmental Decision

     Pursuant to Regulation 18700(c), a public official or
consultant "participates in the making of a governmental decision"
when, acting within the authority of his or her position, he or
she:

     (1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental entity or private
person regarding the decision; or

     (2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

          (A) Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or

          (B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the
part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.

     Presumably, the engineer will be providing information,
advice, recommendation or counsel to the city with respect to

File No. I-90-212
Page 5


geotechnical reports prepared by other consultants.  These reports
are utilized during the permit review process for private
development projects.  If there is "significant intervening
substantive review" of the engineer's recommendations, the
engineer will not be participating in a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(c); Leidigh Advice Letter, No. A-89-320, copy
enclosed.)

However, the Commission has interpreted this exception very
narrowly.  In Kaplan, supra, we stated that:

     A consultant participates in a decision, even
     if it is reviewed by several of his superiors, if
     those superiors rely on the data or analysis
     prepared by the consultant without checking it
     independently, if they rely on the professional
     judgment of the consultant, or if the consultant in
     some other way actually may influence the final
     decision.

You have not provided us with sufficient information about
the relationship between the engineers and the city staff to
determine if there is significant intervening substantive review.
However, under our interpretation, if the city staff makes a
decision on a permit or project and relies upon the engineer's
information or expertise, the engineer is deemed to be
participating in a governmental decision.

Disqualification

An engineer who is a deemed to be a consultant under the Act
is a public official and is subject to the provisions of the Act.
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in,
or using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he or she knows or has reason to know of a
financial interest.  (Section 87100.)  A public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his immediate family or on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

     (b)  Any real property in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect interest worth
     one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

     (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
     and other than loans by a commercial lending
     institution in the regular course of business on
     terms available to the public without regard to

File No. I-90-212
Page 6


     official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
     dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
     received by or promised to the public official
     within 12 months prior to the time when the
     decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

     (e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

     Section 87103(a)-(e).

     As a public official, the engineer may not participate in a
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on any of his or her economic
interests. (Section 87103(a)-(e).) This is a separate analysis
which can be done on a case-by-case basis when the appropriate
facts are provided.

     I trust this answers your questions. If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel


By: Jill R. Stecher
     Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JRS:dg

Enclosures
